title: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Colony Ship as an 'isometric, party-based RPG' with turn-based play, a detailed skill-based character system, multiple ways to handle quests, branching dialogue trees, and choices-and-consequences faction negotiation aboard a generation ship. That combination strongly fits a party-based CRPG: isometric perspective, party control, authored branching quest structure, tactical/methodical turn-based encounters, and heavy dialogue-choice driven progression. Secondary overlap with western_narrative_rpg and tactical_rpg is present, but the party-based isometric CRPG identity is the clearest core gameplay profile.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for hardcore isometric CRPG fans, with several credible neighbors. Best fits emphasize turn-based tactical encounters, dense skill checks, branching quest resolution, and reactive faction-driven progression. The main weakness is that some candidates drift toward more combat-forward or less party/dialogue-centric structures than Colony Ship’s core identity.
anchor_summary: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game is a single-player isometric party-based CRPG with turn-based tactical combat, skill-check-heavy questing, branching dialogue, consequential faction choices, and a hardcore progression/challenge model.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-clone of Colony Ship’s exact mix of party-based isometric CRPG structure, heavy skill gating, and harsh choice-and-consequence design.
Several picks fit the broader hardcore isometric RPG space but differ meaningfully on party structure or campaign format.
The rail leans slightly toward adjacent tactical RPGs and solo isometric RPGs rather than staying tightly centered on party-based reactive CRPGs.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close design lineage: hardcore turn-based CRPG, dense skill-based quest solutions, extensive dialogue, and strong choices-and-consequences structure, though it is less party-centric than Colony Ship.
Classic isometric sci-fi CRPG with branching quests, faction alignment, build-driven skill checks, and tactical turn-based combat; a strong gameplay match despite a somewhat broader/opener structure.

Party-based isometric RPG with reactive story choices, squad building, and turn-based tactical combat; slightly more combat-squad/tactics oriented and less skill-check-centric in moment-to-moment play.

Story-heavy isometric squad RPG with turn-based combat and meaningful dialogue choices, but its hub-and-mission structure is more streamlined and less systemic than Colony Ship’s harsher CRPG-style quest reactivity.

Hardcore isometric turn-based RPG with difficult combat, factions, and strong build expression, but it is more solo exploration/combat-driven and less party-based or dialogue-reactive than Colony Ship.

title: Terra Invicta

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Terra Invicta as a long-form strategic campaign centered on faction-level control of Earth's nations, covert political influence, global research direction, proxy conflicts, Solar System expansion, and base/station construction. The text repeatedly emphasizes geopolitics as a sandbox, detailed regional simulation, councilor management, control points, colonization of asteroids/moons/planets, and tactical fleet combat. That macro-scale political and imperial management profile fits grand_strategy best, with strong 4X overlap from expansion, resource extraction, research, and space colonization. Evidence is rich and concrete across Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic-derived stored text, not merely genre tags.

overall_note: The rail has a solid strategic core, especially with space grand strategy and 4X comps. Best fits are macro-scale sci-fi strategy games with diplomacy, research, expansion, and fleet management. The weakest inclusions either over-index on shared alien-resistance theme rather than gameplay identity, or are represented by a DLC entry instead of the actual comparable base game.
anchor_summary: Terra Invicta is a dense single-player grand strategy/4X hybrid focused on covert faction control of Earth’s nations, research and political influence, Solar System expansion, base/station management, and tactical space-fleet warfare over a long campaign.
gap_notes: Stellaris is the clearest fit in the set and belongs near the top.
Endless Space 2 and Galactic Civilizations IV are good space-4X neighbors, though less geopolitically grounded and less hybridized than Terra Invicta.
XCOM 2 shares anti-alien strategy framing and some strategic layer overlap, but its identity is mission-centric tactical combat rather than grand strategy.
Europa Universalis IV: Dharma is especially awkward because the provided candidate is a DLC, not the base game, and the comparison is looser despite some shared pausable macro strategy DNA.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest overall fit: pausable macro-scale sci-fi strategy with research, diplomacy, expansion, colony/station management, and fleet warfare driving a long campaign.

Strong space 4X match through faction asymmetry, colonization, tech progression, empire management, and large-scale fleet conflict, though it is more classical turn-based 4X than Terra Invicta’s geopolitical simulation.

A credible space-strategy neighbor with research, diplomacy, colonization, and military buildup, but it is a more traditional 4X empire game and less distinctive in political infiltration and Earth-to-Solar-System progression.
Understandable because of the alien-resistance theme and strategic management layer, but the core loop is squad-based turn-based tactical missions, not grand strategy or 4X macro management.

EU4’s grand-strategy DNA is relevant in the abstract, but this specific candidate is the Dharma DLC rather than the base game, and it lacks Terra Invicta’s sci-fi 4X, space expansion, and tactical fleet-combat identity.

title: ARMA 3 Contact

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a spin-off expansion built in 'authentic Arma 3 style,' centered on a military science-fiction campaign with combat recon, electronic warfare, new factions, weapons, vehicles, and a large sandbox terrain suited for combined arms warfare. Store labels also confirm single-player, co-op, and PvP support. The strongest gameplay signals are tactical realism, combat-focused military operations, open sandbox terrain, and methodical shooter play, which fit tactical_shooter best; military_fps is a close secondary fit.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's mil-sim/tactical shooter identity well, especially through other Arma content and a close military-sandbox neighbor. Its main weakness is that it misses the actual ARMA 3 base game while one candidate is another expansion rather than the parent title.
anchor_summary: ARMA 3 Contact is a standalone/expansion-style Arma 3 experience built around realistic tactical military FPS play: large open terrain, combined arms, recon, vehicles, simulation-heavy gunplay, and authored operations/campaign content, with broader Arma sandbox support beyond the campaign.
gap_notes: The strongest expected comp is the ARMA 3 base game itself, but it is not actually present in the candidate set.

title: RimWorld - Royalty

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This is additive DLC for RimWorld and does not contradict the parent game's core identity, so the parent gameplay should drive classification. Parent text explicitly describes RimWorld as a 'sci-fi colony sim' where you manage colonists' needs and relationships, build and craft a settlement, trade, tame animals, form caravans, and respond to raids and other systemic threats. Royalty adds quests, psychic powers, noble titles, throne rooms, hospitality scenarios, and more combat/event variety, but these extend the same colony-management and story-generation loop rather than establishing a different genre. The combined evidence strongly supports colony_sim with systemic sandbox management, emergent narrative, settlement building, colony growth, and tactical encounter handling.

overall_note: The set includes the essential base game and several genuinely strong colony-sim neighbors. Ordering mostly hinges on how closely each game matches RimWorld's pawn-driven survival/settlement loop and tactical threat handling versus being a more general management sim.
anchor_summary: RimWorld - Royalty is additive DLC for RimWorld, so the core similarity target is the same colony-sim sandbox: top-down/indirect management of individual pawns with needs and traits, base building, resource production, emergent story events, raid defense, and tactical pauseable combat. Strong comps should match that colony management loop more than just broad strategy/sim overlap.
gap_notes: RimWorld should be the clear must-include because Royalty is direct DLC.
Dwarf Fortress, Stranded: Alien Dawn, and Going Medieval all fit as strong colony-sim neighbors, with Dwarf Fortress the most canonical benchmark.
Prison Architect is a reasonable adjacent management comp, but it is less aligned on survival expedition/combat and colony-sandbox progression.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game and identical core colony-sim loop; Royalty is an expansion layered onto RimWorld's exact systems and audience.
Foundational emergent colony sim with individual inhabitants, construction, survival pressures, and systemic story generation; an obvious benchmark for RimWorld players.
Very close small-group survival colony sim: manage survivors' needs, build/expand a base, research tech, and defend against periodic external threats.

Top-down building/management sim with emergent incidents and staffing logistics, but it lacks RimWorld's survival-colony loop, world exploration, and comparable tactical combat emphasis.

title: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as grand_strategy using DLC inheritance from the parent game Total War: THREE KINGDOMS, since Eight Princes is clearly an additive chapter pack rather than a genre-shifting mode. The DLC text repeatedly describes new campaign mechanics, princes, buildings, assignments, court options, faction advancement, alignments, and elite units, which matches the parent game's core loop. Parent evidence is strong and explicit: THREE KINGDOMS combines a turn-based campaign of empire-building, statecraft, and conquest with real-time battles. That supports grand_strategy as the primary identity, with rts as a secondary flavor for battlefield engagements. Historical setting, long-form campaign structure, systemic faction management, settlement development, and tactical optimization are all directly supported by the stored descriptions.

overall_note: The set is strong and correctly centers the base game plus very close Total War neighbors. Ordering mostly hinges on how directly each title matches the Three Kingdoms-era campaign/battle hybrid and how close it is in structure rather than setting.
anchor_summary: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes is a chapter-pack DLC for Total War: THREE KINGDOMS, preserving the series' core identity: turn-based grand-strategy campaign management with diplomacy, faction development, and empire building, paired with real-time tactical battles. Best matches should share that exact hybrid campaign/battle loop, long-form strategic structure, and army-scale tactical combat; direct base-game linkage matters most here.

title: Battle Cry of Freedom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Battle Cry of Freedom as a large-scale multiplayer PvP combat game centered on historically grounded Civil War battles with first- and third-person gunplay, artillery, cavalry, engineer support, melee bayonet/sabre fighting, realistic projectile physics, destructible battlefields, and commander-led tactical play. The strongest identity is tactical shooter: match-based battlefield engagements, realism-leaning weapon behavior, role-based military units, and emphasis on tactical cunning over narrative or RPG progression. It also overlaps with military_fps due to historical armed combat and firearm focus, but the scale, role variety, and tactical realism make tactical_shooter the better primary fit. Evidence for MMO-like scale exists in store genres and 500-600 player battles, but not enough to justify an MMO RPG archetype.

overall_note: The set contains one very strong historical tactical-shooter comp and one broader combat sandbox neighbor. It covers the anchor's realism-leaning battlefield combat reasonably well, but misses an even closer large-scale line-battle or Napoleonic/Civil War multiplayer benchmark.

title: AI War 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify AI War 2 as a 'grand strategy/RTS hybrid' centered on galactic-scale territorial expansion, fleet and defense-network management, multiple factions with unique economies and rules, and real-time simulation with pause/speed controls. The core loop is long-form campaign play where the player starts from a tiny foothold, expands carefully, manages fronts, optimizes empire and fleet composition, and eventually strikes key AI leaders. The text repeatedly emphasizes macro decision-making, automation, tactical optimization, and large-scale systemic simulation rather than match-based or action-oriented play, which strongly fits grand_strategy with RTS and 4X overlap.

overall_note: The rail has two credible space-strategy neighbors, but it misses the anchor's unusually strong RTS-with-pause and automation-heavy identity. title: Killer7

title: The Eternal Cylinder

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently depict a single-player, third-person 3D open-world survival adventure centered on exploring an alien planet, evading a world-destroying cylinder, solving environmental puzzles, and adapting a herd via mutations that unlock traversal and survival abilities. The core loop is exploration, survival under pressure, puzzle-solving, and story progression rather than shooter combat or RPG party tactics. Repeated mentions of a procedurally generated ecosystem, emergent gameplay, real-time world destruction, rescuing and growing the herd, and mutation-based adaptation strongly support exploration_survival_adventure.

overall_note: The rail has a solid top end built around vulnerable-creature survival and adaptation-driven exploration, but it weakens notably in the lower half with looser action/exploration comps and one clearly incorrect candidate record.
anchor_summary: The Eternal Cylinder is a single-player 3D exploration-survival adventure focused on traversing a strange alien world as a vulnerable creature herd, using mutation-based adaptation to unlock traversal and survival options, evading large-scale environmental threats and predators, and progressing through story and environmental puzzles rather than combat-heavy action.

title: Paper Dolls 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify Paper Dolls 2 as a first-person horror adventure set in a haunted Qing-dynasty mansion, centered on exploration, unraveling a buried mystery, solving puzzles, collecting clues/messages, and surviving or avoiding hostile spirits. The copy explicitly says players must 'avoid, shake off, and defeat' lost souls, which supports some light survival-horror interaction, but combat specifics are sparse and do not clearly establish action-forward combat. The strongest identity is therefore psychological horror: first-person, single-player, authored campaign, oppressive haunted-house exploration, mystery-solving, and survival-escape framing, with survival_horror as a secondary influence.

title: Amazing Cultivation Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Official store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify Amazing Cultivation Simulator as a strategy sim/management game inspired by RimWorld and Dwarf Fortress. Core play centers on designing and expanding a cultivation sect, managing disciples, researching knowledge, building infrastructure, handling commerce/political relations, and surviving a procedurally generated world with changing NPC sect interactions. Combat and threats exist (spirits, monsters, demons, beast sieges), but they support a broader settlement/colony-management loop rather than defining the game as an action RPG. The strongest fit in the taxonomy is colony_sim, with management_tycoon and city_builder as secondary influences.

title: Total War: WARHAMMER - Realm of The Wood Elves

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This title is clearly additive DLC for Total War: WARHAMMER and does not contradict the parent game's core gameplay, so parent evidence is primary. The parent description explicitly defines the loop as 'turn-based campaign of epic empire-building with explosive, colossal, real-time battles,' which strongly supports an RTS-primary strategy identity with grand-strategy overlap. The DLC description preserves that structure while adding a new race, campaign map, faction currency, building/wonder victory condition, outposts, tech/building infrastructure, lord/hero skill trees, and large-scale real-time battles with ranged, melee, and magic units. Together this supports a long-form tactical-overhead strategy game centered on real-time command, settlement/infrastructure building, faction growth, and campaign-level conquest.

overall_note: The set contains the required base game and only very close Total War: WARHAMMER-family comps. Ordering mainly depends on whether exact same-platform DLC similarity beats sequel-level continuity.
anchor_summary: Race-pack DLC for Total War: WARHAMMER built on the series' defining hybrid loop: turn-based campaign management and expansion paired with large-scale real-time tactical battles. Similarity should prioritize other Total War: WARHAMMER content that preserves that exact campaign/battle structure, faction asymmetry, lord/hero progression, and army-scale combat.
gap_notes: No obvious off-genre candidates; all four belong in the same gameplay family.
The strongest editorial question is ranking another same-engine race-pack DLC versus the later standalone sequels.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game for this DLC, with the identical turn-based campaign plus real-time battle structure, progression systems, and content dependency relationship editors would expect.
